title: Setting the hot water cylinder thermostat
subtitle: The 60°C rule, legionella control, and how to balance energy savings with safety
excerpt: A hot water cylinder thermostat controls the maximum stored water temperature. The standard setting of 60°C comes from legionella control requirements: at 60°C, Legionella bacteria die within two minutes. Lower settings save energy but create a legionella risk in stored water. The compromise used in most heat-pump installs is 55°C daily with a weekly pasteurisation cycle to 60°C.
sourceNotes: ACOP L8 and HSG274 Part 2 for legionella control in hot water systems. CIBSE TM13 on avoiding legionella in building services. WHO Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality 4th edition for Legionella temperature thresholds. Energy Saving Trust hot water guidance. Vaillant and Worcester Bosch cylinder installation manuals for thermostat calibration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A device that uses electricity to move heat from outside air or the ground. Heat pumps are less efficient at high cylinder temperatures, so the temperature setting is a key operating decision.

content.text: A hot water cylinder thermostat sits in a pocket in the cylinder wall and controls when the heat source (boiler, heat pump, or immersion heater) fires to reheat the stored water. Most UK cylinders are factory-set to 60°C. This temperature is set for a reason: Legionella bacteria multiply in water between 20°C and 45°C and are killed in under two minutes at 60°C. Below 60°C, the kill time rises: at 55°C it takes about 5-6 hours of continuous exposure for the same effect.

For most homes, the legionella risk from a cylinder is low. The water turns over frequently, the cylinder is small, and there are no complex circuits. ACOP L8 explicitly excludes domestic premises from formal risk-assessment. The 60°C default is the safest option. A managed 55°C plus weekly pasteurisation is widely used in heat-pump installs for the efficiency gain.
For a gas boiler-heated cylinder, the energy consumption difference between 55°C and 60°C is small (around 3-5% of the standby heat loss through the cylinder walls). For a

the difference is larger: a COP of 2.8 at 60°C vs 3.5 at 55°C. Over a year with a 200-litre cylinder charged daily, the saving from 55°C vs 60°C on a heat-pump system is around 150-250 kWh/year, worth a modest cost at 27p/kWh.
Set the cylinder thermostat to 55°C for daily operation. This is where the heat pump is most efficient.
Program the immersion heater or boiler to heat the cylinder to 60°C once per week (typically overnight, on a cheap electricity tariff). Hold at 60°C for at least one hour.
Fit a thermostatic blending valve at the cylinder outlet, set to 50°C. This prevents scalding at the taps during and after the pasteurisation cycle.

Turn off the electricity before opening the thermostat cover. Most immersion thermostats have a slotted screw that sets the temperature (marked on the dial). Restore power and check the stored temperature at the hot outlet with a contact thermometer after one full heating cycle.
